title: Prostate Health. A Grape-Enriched Diet Attenuated Obesity-Related Inflammation and Prostate Cancer Progression. The chemopreventive potential of grape-supplemented diets was evaluated in genetically predisposed and obesity-provoked prostate cancer, using prostate-specific Pten heterozygous transgenic mice. The animals were fed a low- and high-fat diet, supplemented with 10 percent grape powder for 33 weeks. Consuming grapes had a beneficial impact: researchers observed a reduction in proliferation of prostate epithelial cells, rescue of P T E N expression, a protective effect against high-fat diet induced inflammation, activation of cell survival pathways and angiogenesis, as well as reduced circulating levels of cancer-promoting micro R N As.

The animals were fed a low- and high-fat diet, supplemented with 10 percent grape powder for 33 weeks.

Consuming grapes had a beneficial impact: researchers observed a reduction in proliferation of prostate epithelial cells, rescue of P T E N expression, a protective effect against high-fat diet induced inflammation, activation of cell survival pathways and angiogenesis, as well as reduced circulating levels of cancer-promoting micro R N As.
